1: "Jacob also went on his way, and the angels of God met him." The angels of God (malʾakê ʾĕlōhîm) appearing immediately after Laban's departure creates a divine-escort narrative that mirrors the Bethel theophany at Jacob's departure from Canaan (Genesis 28:12: "angels of God ascending and descending on it"). The covenant-journey began and ends with angelic encounter: Jacob left Canaan with a vision of divine protection; he returns to Canaan with divine escort. The angels here do not speak — their presence is the message. The covenant-bearer approaches the promised land under YHWH's visible protection, not merely his abstract promise.
2: "This is the camp of God! So he named that place Mahanaim." Mahanaim (maḥănayim, dual form of maḥăneh, camp) means "two camps" — Jacob's camp and YHWH's camp. The divine military camp alongside Jacob's human camp is the warrior-escort imagery: YHWH's forces accompany the covenant-family into the land. The Mahanaim site will later be the location where David fled Absalom (2 Samuel 17:24,27) and where Solomon's administrative district included Mahanaim (1 Kings 4:14). The name-giving formula grounds a known Transjordanian place-name in Jacob's encounter with the divine army.
3: "Jacob sent messengers (malʾākîm) ahead of him to his brother Esau." The word malʾākîm (messengers/angels) is the same word as the divine angels of vv1-2. The verbal echo is deliberate: just as divine messengers escorted Jacob, human messengers go ahead of him. Jacob the supplanter now organizes his return diplomatically, acknowledging the covenant-complication that Esau represents. "The land of Seir, the country of Edom" — Seir/Edom is the Transjordanian/Arabian territory south of the Dead Sea where Esau settled (Genesis 36); at this point in the narrative Esau has moved there permanently, suggesting significant time has passed since his Canaanite marriages (Genesis 26).
4: "Your servant Jacob says, I have been staying with Laban and have remained there till now." The "your servant (ʿaḇdĕkā)" formula is the diplomatic self-humbling before a superior — Jacob places himself in the client position before Esau. The message intentionally omits mention of YHWH's blessing or Jacob's wealth accumulation — leading with "I have been staying with Laban" presents Jacob as a returning family member, not a rival. The phrase "remained there till now (wāʾēḥar ʿaḏ-ʿattāh)" acknowledges the long absence without explaining it as flight — diplomatic understatement.
5: "I have cattle and donkeys, sheep and goats, male and female servants." The inventory of Jacob's wealth serves two diplomatic functions: (1) it demonstrates that Jacob is not returning to claim Esau's inheritance or blessing — he is independently wealthy; (2) it signals his capacity to give gifts if the meeting goes well. "That I may find favor (ḥēn) in your eyes" — the patron-client favor-seeking formula of the weaker party before the stronger. Jacob's self-presentation as wealthy-but-humble is a calculated diplomatic posture: too poor and Esau might be contemptuous; too proud and Esau might be provoked.
6: "Now he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him." The messengers' report transforms Jacob's diplomatic initiative into a military crisis. Four hundred men is a significant fighting force — the same number that rallied around David in distress (1 Samuel 22:2) and around Nabal (1 Samuel 25:13). Whether Esau is coming as a welcoming party or a war party is ambiguous from the number alone — Jacob assumes the worst (v7: "in great fear and distress"). The report inverts the power dynamic: Jacob sent a message of conciliation; Esau responds with an army.
7: "In great fear and distress Jacob divided the people who were with him into two groups." Jacob's military division is the "Mahanaim strategy" — two camps, if one is struck the other escapes (v8). The vocabulary of fear — wayyîrāʾ yaʿăqōḇ mĕʾōḏ wayyēṣer lô (Jacob was very afraid and he was distressed) — uses two fear-words: yārēʾ (to be afraid) and ṣûr (to be pressed/distressed). The doubled fear-vocabulary marks this as the narrative's most intense anxiety. The practical wisdom of the military division shows Jacob operating simultaneously on two tracks: the covenant-faith he will express in prayer (v9-12) and the human strategy he employs in planning.
8: "If Esau comes and attacks one group, the group that is left may escape." The two-camp survival strategy mirrors the Mahanaim vision (two camps = Jacob's + God's): the physical two-camp division reflects the spiritual two-camp reality. The strategy is sound ANE military planning: dividing assets reduced total loss risk. The word "escape (lĕpelēṭāh)" is the covenant-remnant vocabulary — the same root as pālaṭ (to escape/deliver) used throughout the prophets for Israel's surviving remnant. Jacob's practical military calculation uses the language of covenant-survival.
9: "O God of my father Abraham, God of my father Isaac, LORD, who said to me, 'Go back to your country and your relatives, and I will make you prosper.'" Jacob's prayer is the most theologically developed prayer in Genesis. The triple divine address — God of Abraham + God of Isaac + YHWH — covers the full covenant-genealogy: personal covenant-history from grandfather to father to the covenant-name. Jacob grounds his petition in divine speech: "who said to me" — he is holding YHWH to his own word (Genesis 31:3). Prayer that appeals to God's stated promise is covenant-prayer at its most confident.
10: "I am unworthy of all the kindness and faithfulness (ḥesed wĕʾĕmet) you have shown your servant. I had only my staff when I crossed this Jordan, but now I have become two camps." The prayer's self-abasement is theologically precise: qāṭōntî mikōl-haḥăsādîm — "I am too small for all the loyal-loves" — the word qāṭōn (too small/insignificant) is a superlative self-diminishment. The covenant word-pair ḥesed wĕʾĕmet (loyal-love and faithfulness, also Genesis 24:27; Genesis 47:29) summarizes YHWH's character in covenant-relationship. "Only my staff (bĕmaqlĕlî)" — the single traveler's staff is the image of starting with nothing; "two camps" is the image of abundant covenant-blessing. The contrast measures YHWH's faithfulness.
11: "Save me, I pray, from the hand of my brother Esau, for I am afraid he will come and attack me, and also the mothers with their children." The petition specifies the threat — not just Jacob personally but the mothers and children (i.e., the next generation of the covenant-family). The phrase "mothers with their children (ʾēm ʿal-bānîm)" is the most vulnerable element of the camp: attacking mothers with nursing children was the paradigm of military barbarism in the OT (2 Kings 8:12; Hosea 10:14; Amos 1:13). Jacob's prayer locates the covenant's continuity in the vulnerable children — the future of the promise depends on their survival.
12: "But you have said, 'I will surely make you prosper and will make your descendants like the sand of the sea, which cannot be counted.'" The prayer closes with the covenant-promise as its ultimate ground. Jacob quotes YHWH's word (Genesis 31:3: "I will be with you"; Genesis 28:14-15: the Bethel promise) back to YHWH — not as a demand but as a trust-anchor. The "sand of the sea" imagery for descendants is the Abrahamic covenant-vocabulary (Genesis 22:17; Genesis 13:16). The appeal to YHWH's promise is the most powerful form of covenant-prayer: God's faithfulness to his own word is the basis of the petition, not Jacob's worthiness.
13: "He spent the night there, and from what he had with him he selected a gift for his brother Esau." The night between prayer and action is structurally significant: Jacob prays (vv9-12) and then plans (vv13-21), not instead of praying but after praying. The gift-selection is a calculated appeasement strategy. In ANE diplomacy, a gift (minḥāh) sent ahead of a delegation was the standard method of softening a superior before a face-to-face meeting. The word minḥāh is also the word for "offering" — the gift to Esau uses the same vocabulary as a sacrificial offering, suggesting Jacob is treating the encounter with quasi-religious seriousness.
14: Two hundred female goats and twenty male goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams — the first installment of the gift inventory. The ratio of female-to-male (Genesis 10:1) in each category maximizes reproductive value: female animals represent ongoing herd-building capacity, while males are the breeding stock. A gift structured for future productivity rather than mere present value shows sophisticated economic thinking. The totals are substantial: 220 goats, 220 sheep — portions of a large flock, not token gifts.
15: Thirty female camels with their young, forty cows and ten bulls, twenty female donkeys and ten male donkeys — the second installment. The camels "with their young (wĕyalḏêhen)" are the most valuable single line: a nursing female camel with her young doubled the economic value of each animal. The selection of the most valuable animals in each category shows the scale of Jacob's appeasement investment. The total gift (vv14-15) would represent hundreds of animals — an enormous wealth-transfer by any ancient standard, designed to demonstrate that Jacob has not come to compete with Esau's resources.
16: "He put them in the care of his servants, each herd by itself, and said to his servants, 'Go ahead of me, and keep some space between the herds.'" The spacing of the gift-herds is a psychological strategy: Esau will encounter gift after gift in waves, each wave building anticipation and softening reaction before Jacob himself appears. The "space between the herds (rewah tāśîmû bên ʿēḏer ûḇên ʿēḏer)" — both the word rewah (space/relief) and the spacing concept recall Rehoboth (Genesis 26:22: "the LORD has made room for us"). The waves of gifts create expanding space between Esau's hostility and Jacob's approach.
17: "When my brother Esau meets you and asks, 'Who do you belong to, and where are you going, and who owns all these animals in front of you?'" The scripted servant-reply anticipates Esau's interrogation of each gift-wave. The three questions Esau will ask — identity, destination, ownership — are the standard questions for a border-crossing challenge in the ANE. Jacob's pre-scripted answers ensure consistent messaging across all the gift-waves: each servant will give the same reply, reinforcing the narrative of a humble Jacob sending gifts to his master Esau.
18: "They belong to your servant Jacob; they are a gift sent to my lord Esau, and he is coming behind us." The scripted reply repeats the servant/lord designation at least three times across vv18-20 — a deliberate reinforcement of Jacob's self-humbling posture. "He is coming behind us (gam-hinnēh ʾaḥărênû)" positions Jacob as following his gifts, not leading — the physical position of the suppliant before the patron. The sequential staging (gifts first, Jacob last) reverses the normal order of a dignitary's procession, where the principal comes first.
19: "He also instructed the second, the third and all the others who followed the herds: 'You are to say the same thing to Esau when you meet him.'" The repeated scripting of each group of servants creates redundancy as a rhetorical strategy: five separate waves of gift-herds, each with its own servant giving the same rehearsed message, creates the cumulative effect of five separate encounters each saying the same thing. The ANE audience would recognize this as a classic diplomatic softening campaign — the repeated message, the repeated gifts, the repeated "your servant" designation all working in concert.
20: "I will pacify him with these gifts I am sending on ahead; later, when I see him, perhaps he will receive me." The word "pacify" (ʾăkappĕrāh pānāyw) is the verb kipper — the same root as kippûr, the Day of Atonement. Jacob's diplomatic strategy uses atonement vocabulary: the gifts are meant to "cover/atone" Esau's face (his anger). "Perhaps (ʾûlay) he will receive me (yiśśāʾ pānāy)" — the phrase "lift up my face" is the idiom for acceptance and favor (the priestly blessing: "the LORD lift up his countenance upon you," Numbers 6:26). Jacob hopes Esau will "lift his face toward him" in acceptance rather than hostility.
21: "So Jacob's gifts went on ahead of him, but he himself spent the night in the camp." The night Jacob spends in the camp after sending gifts and family ahead is the night of the wrestling match (vv22-31). The structural pattern is significant: Jacob has done everything humanly possible (prayer, military preparation, diplomatic gifts) and now faces the night alone. The stripping away of all human strategies and resources — gifts gone, family gone, alone at the ford — is the narrative's preparation for the divine encounter that transforms him.
22: "That night Jacob got up and took his two wives, his two female servants and his eleven sons and crossed the ford of the Jabbok." The Jabbok river (modern Zarqa) is a significant Transjordanian boundary stream flowing westward into the Jordan. The name Yabbōq sounds like both yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob) and yēʾāḇēq (to wrestle) — a triple wordplay woven into the geography. The "ford (maʿăḇar)" was a shallow crossing point; the text specifies Jacob moved his entire household across before returning alone. The Jabbok crossing will become a type of the Jordan crossing into Canaan: both are water-boundary crossings that mark transformation.
23: "After he had sent them across the stream, he sent over all his possessions." The progressive sending-across — family first, then possessions — leaves Jacob completely alone on the Jabbok's eastern bank. The reversal from the earlier military division (divide the camp for protection) to this complete exposure (send everyone across, stay alone) is stark: Jacob is now completely unprotected, having sent away his defense. The solitude is not carelessness but a deliberate positioning — or perhaps the narrative's irony: having arranged every human protection possible, Jacob is left alone for the one encounter no human strategy could arrange.
24: "So Jacob was left alone, and a man wrestled with him till daybreak." "A man (ʾîš)" is the text's deliberately ambiguous identification of the wrestling figure. Jacob identifies the encounter as seeing God face to face (v30); Hosea 12:4 identifies the figure as "the angel" (malʾāk); the text says "a man." The ambiguity is the encounter's mystery: a divine figure who takes human form, like the three men at Mamre (Genesis 18) and the man with the drawn sword before Joshua (Joshua 5:13-15). The wrestling continues "until the breaking of the day (ʿaḏ ʿălôt haššāḥar)" — an all-night physical contest with a divine figure who does not reveal himself in the dark.
25: "When the man saw that he could not overpower him, he touched the socket of Jacob's hip so that his hip was wrenched." The wrestling figure's inability to overpower Jacob — and the subsequent hip-touch — reveals the encounter's theological logic: the divine figure could end the match at will (as the hip-touch demonstrates: a single touch dislocates Jacob's hip) but chooses not to. The match continues at Jacob's persistence. The hip socket (kap-yĕrēk, the hollow of the thigh — the joint of the hip) is the same yārēk (thigh/loin) used in the oath-by-thigh custom (Genesis 24:2; Genesis 47:29). The wrenching of the covenant-oath body-part is a bodily transformation.
26: "Let me go, for it is daybreak. But Jacob replied, 'I will not let you go unless you bless me.'" The daybreak deadline — the divine figure asking to leave before dawn — may reflect the ANE convention that divine figures operate in the liminal spaces of night and that exposure to daylight ends the encounter's sacred ambiguity. Jacob's refusal to release without a blessing is the narrative's theological climax: the man who spent his life maneuvering for blessings (Esau's birthright, Isaac's blessing) now demands blessing directly from God himself. The demand is not presumptuous but persistent faith — grasping for the divine blessing the way he grasped Esau's heel at birth.
27: "What is your name? 'Jacob,' he answered." The question of the name — coming from the divine figure — echoes Isaac's question in Genesis 27:18 ("Who are you, my son?"), where Jacob lied and said "Esau." The false identity claim obtained Isaac's blessing; the honest name-declaration here obtains the divine blessing. Jacob cannot receive the transforming blessing with a false name: he must come to God as he actually is — Jacob, the heel-grabber, the supplanter — before he can become Israel. The honest answer to "what is your name?" is the condition for the name-change that follows.
28: "Your name will no longer be Jacob, but Israel, because you have struggled with God and with humans and have overcome." The name Israel (yiśrāʾēl) is explained via the etymology "he struggles with God (śārāh ʾel) and prevails (yûkāl)." The name change from Jacob (Heel-grabber, Supplanter — yaʿăqōḇ) to Israel (One-who-strives-with-God — yiśrāʾēl) is the covenant-family's founding moment: the man becomes the nation's name. "Struggled with God and with humans" encompasses Jacob's entire narrative arc: his wrestling with Esau, Laban, and YHWH are all instances of the same character-quality — persistent, tenacious engagement that refuses to let go without a blessing. The nation Israel inherits both the name and the character.
29: "Please tell me your name. But he replied, 'Why do you ask my name?' Then he blessed him there." The divine figure's refusal to give his name follows the pattern of Manoah's inquiry of the angel (Judges 13:17-18: "why do you ask my name? It is beyond understanding"). In ANE thought, knowing a being's true name gave power over it — the divine refuses to be named and thereby controlled. The blessing without self-disclosure maintains the theological asymmetry: Jacob has wrestled with and been blessed by a being he cannot name or fully comprehend. The blessing itself is the gift; the name remains mystery.
30: "It is because I saw God face to face, and yet my life was spared." The naming of Peniel/Penuel (pĕnîʾēl, face of God) interprets the encounter as a divine face-to-face meeting. The expectation that seeing God's face brings death (Exodus 33:20: "you cannot see my face, for no one may see me and live") makes Jacob's survival the narrative's statement of extraordinary grace. "My life was spared (wattinnāṣēl napšî, my life was delivered)" — the verb nāṣal (to deliver/rescue) is the Exodus deliverance vocabulary. Encountering God is both the most dangerous and most life-giving thing that can happen — and Jacob has experienced both in one night.
31: "The sun rose above him as he passed Peniel, and he was limping because of his hip." The sunrise marks the end of the liminal night-encounter and the beginning of the new day — Jacob/Israel's first sunrise. The limp (ṣōlēaʿ, limping) is the permanent physical mark of the night: Jacob has been changed bodily by the encounter. The limping patriarch approaching his brother with his hosts — wounded, walking unevenly, but carrying a new name — is the narrative's image of covenant-transformation: God's blessing does not always leave the recipient without marks. The limp is not a defeat but the cost of prevailing.
32: "Therefore to this day the Israelites do not eat the tendon attached to the socket of the hip (gîd hanāšeh), because the socket of Jacob's hip was touched near the tendon." The gîd hanāšeh (sciatic nerve/hip tendon prohibition) is one of the oldest Jewish dietary laws, predating the Mosaic dietary code of Leviticus and Deuteronomy. The "to this day (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh)" etiological formula grounds a known Jewish practice in the founding ancestor's experience. The prohibition preserves the memory of Jacob's encounter as a bodily practice: every time the sciatic nerve is removed from an animal (still required in kosher butchering), the act recalls the night at the Jabbok. The wound becomes communal memory through dietary law.
